summary: The interview begins with introductions, then the interviewer asks the employee to describe a technical project they are proud of. The employee details a submission microservice, focusing on its resilience, performance, and the architectural decisions made under a tight deadline. The discussion covers the employee's specific contributions, API design for an offline mode, service placement in the overall system, SLAs, and the rationale behind choosing S3 for intermediate storage. The employee discusses his role as a release manager, detailing challenges in cross-team communication and coordination for releases, and how he overcame them through overcommunication and process improvements. He also describes the automation used in the release process. The interviewer then asks about potential improvements he would make to the projects, to which the employee suggests giving teams more autonomy in release schedules. The chunk concludes with the interviewer wrapping up the interview and then a debrief session with another person (Pasa) discussing the candidate's performance, focusing on themes like ownership, passion, communication style, and delivery. This chunk features a discussion between two interviewers (one being 'Persona') and Jonathan (the 'Employee' whose interview is being reviewed). They analyze Jonathan's personal pitch, focusing on how candidates can effectively convey seniority and project complexity, especially those without 'name brand' company experience. Key topics include the importance of mentioning years of experience, leadership roles, diverse experience, and using 'breadcrumbs' in a pitch to guide follow-up questions. They also discuss the role of metrics and how to showcase complexity for smaller-scale projects.
quality_flags: The 'Employee' (Jonathan) in this chunk often participates in the discussion as an expert/panelist rather than solely as the candidate being interviewed, limiting direct Q&A pairs for training.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: All right, let's get started. So, let's talk about something that you're proud of. Pick any technical project that you've done recently or maybe in the last couple years that you're really proud of and you're okay sharing with me today.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. One project that I'm really proud of is from being on the team that has a microservice architecture. We have our web client where people fill in forms and click buttons. We have our main API which stores and records some information, and we also have a few other services. The important one that I worked on is the submission microservice, which is responsible for taking that data that someone has given and sending it for them to process. The reason I'm so proud of this project is because of the timeline, which was really tight, and a lot of the systems that I introduced there. I joined at a time when we were really close to launching, about two months before we were supposed to go public, and we were in a rush to productionize everything. We had the systems working and acting, but we had to make sure that there wasn't any issue. We're working with really important, critical information, and you don't want to make a mistake. So, here's what I did to productionize the system. We had a few things that we wanted to make sure worked. One, we wanted to make sure our system could recover if something goes wrong. You don't want to lose someone's data. Two, we wanted to make sure that we don't overwhelm the downstream APIs that we interact with. We have the information; we have to send it out to the IRS, and we don't want to overload the system or hit rate limits. The final thing that I did after working on resilience and handling downstream issues was performance. So, here's what I did for those things. For recovery, I wanted to make sure that we store the data in a temporary area. So, we read that information from a queue, we temporarily store it in object storage. That means if something goes down, we still have it available for us. To talk about handling downstream API outages, we introduced what we call online-offline mode, but it's more or less like a circuit breaker pattern. This basically means we're processing, and if we get an error, we want to hold off on sending any more requests to that downstream system and then periodically ping it to see if this thing is still up. Another way we tried to handle that, to make sure we weren't overwhelming the API, was batching up our requests. So, instead of sending 100 requests with one return, we want to make sure that we send 100 returns in a single request. Batching those up is also one way to avoid overwhelming the API with tons and tons of requests. Ultimately, the changes that I made helped ensure that we were able to support all the returns. At the end of filing season, we were able to successfully file over 140,000 returns, and even across downstream outages or restarts, emergency deploys, we didn't lose any information.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: That sounds like a really cool service. I'm sure I probably used your service too. That's so cool. You hit close to home and talked about performance and resilience, but we'll talk more about that. I want to understand what was your role going into the project? You mentioned you were two months out from delivery. What was the state of the system when you joined and what did you specifically contribute to it?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Absolutely. So, when I joined, we had a system that was able to interact with the downstream APIs, which is obviously table stakes. But here are the things that I introduced. One was the system of batching. So, instead of just reading messages from the queue and sending them to the downstream API, I introduced batching so that when those messages come in, we're going to queue them up into batches of 100, for example, and once we fill that bucket up, we're going to send that information down to the API. The other thing that I implemented was that offline mode, that circuit breaker that I mentioned. So, the main idea there is that as we're processing, we might occasionally get errors. The API might say, "Hey, we're down. Hey, there was an issue processing things." So, I was responsible for implementing the system that said, "Okay, we're going to pause our processing and we're just going to go into an offline mode where we'll periodically ping the API to make sure that it's back up." Likewise, I was also responsible for some of the error handling there. So, you have a situation where the API was down. We're holding off on submitting those returns because we want to make sure things are working. So, when it comes back up, I implemented that error handling system to pick up any stuff that should have gone out to the API but didn't because we were waiting for it to be online.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Pretty cool. Can you talk a little bit more about the API design itself? Let's talk about the offline system that you designed and implemented. What were the API constructs like, what were the agreements, and how did you think about coming up with those contracts?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. So, for the offline mode, there are a few things that we have to take into consideration. I'll talk about two parts of it, and we can go into more depth as needed. One part of that is we're still taking in those returns. We have a microservice architecture. We're using queues to decouple those applications. So, we're still reading in messages from our queue which tell us this is the information we want to send down, and then we also have that downstream API that sends us that information, and we might get an acknowledgment back. So, let's talk about the first part for supporting that offline mode. The first part of that, like I mentioned, was where we're storing that information in batches. So, the first part of that was making sure that even if we know the API is down, we're still pulling in that information and putting it in temporary storage. For that, I used S3 to temporarily store that information. In terms of the circuit breaker pattern specifically, here's what we would do when you submit that information to the API. The API will give you two things. One, it will either give you an acknowledgment, "Hey, we received the information," or it might give you an error response. The IRS provides a bunch of docs about what those different error codes mean. So, we were looking for very specific error codes that tell us, "Hey, we weren't able to process this because there was an issue downstream." So, the first thing we do is say there was an error downstream. The first thing that I did for that was, when I send that information down to the API and I get that particular error code, I kicked off a piece of logic that basically says, "I got this error code, and now I want to turn off submissions to that API." So, at that point, the main thing that I turned off was just that submission part. We had an internal queuing system within the service, and what that just means is we'll do all the pre-processing, but we're just going to hold off. The other final part of that that I mentioned is that ping to the API. So, we have a background job that, when our offline mode is enabled, I set a configurable number of minutes that we would wait to ping the API again. I don't remember the exact number; it might have been three minutes, five minutes, something to that effect. And once we finally get a positive response, I enable our system to continue processing those submissions and sending them to the API.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: All right. So, just to understand where your service is in the entire flow, is it the first service that when a user uploads, does it go to your service first, or are there other services before they call into you?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: There's one service before it calls into me. So, our architecture is we have a web client that someone interacts with. We have a traditional API. Someone will be filling out their form on the UI, they might be clicking buttons, whatever the case may be. When they're finally ready to click submit, at that point, a request goes to the API, does some pre-processing, and then it puts a message on a message queue. We used to put it on an SQS queue, and then my application is reading messages off of the queue, doing a bit of processing based on some requirements, and then sending it directly to the API that does the actual processing. There are a few other downstream services as well, you know, to make sure it was rejected, to send emails out. There are a few other pieces, but my part was that second step. You hit submit, a message goes on the queue. My service reads that message from the queue, does some pre-processing, and submits it to that API.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: All right. Okay. Now that makes more sense. Thank you. One question, maybe quickly on the technical side. Was there a time criticality or an SLA that your service had to meet, like having to respond within so many seconds, or was that not time critical?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: In this case, we didn't have a hard SLA for my specific service, but as a product, we wanted to make sure that someone got a response within an hour. The reason I mentioned that is because the API itself was also asynchronous. When I submit it, it doesn't say this thing was accepted or rejected immediately. It gives me an acknowledgment. So, it says we've received it. We acknowledge that. And once we have that acknowledgment, we can later use that ID they give to say was this accepted, was this rejected. I don't know that they publish any specific numbers on how that API performs, but we make sure to store that information so that we can later check at some regular cadence, "Hey, have you processed this finally after you gave us this acknowledgment?"

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Cool. Did you have to make any architectural decisions while you were implementing and designing this stuff? And if so, can you walk me through how you made those decisions?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Certainly. So, one decision that we had to decide between, or one decision that I had to decide between, was where to store this intermediate information. One option was to just take advantage of S3 and just use that for keeping track of what returns still need to be processed, as well as storing that information temporarily. The other option was that I could do a split approach where I use a database instead to handle that batching. What I mean by that is one common pattern that you might see if you're processing things in batches with a relational database is I might have a database table that doesn't store all the information. It'll just store a URL that references where the information is, and you can essentially treat your database as a queue and have multiple different instances that kind of read, block a couple of rows, process them, and send it around. The other option was instead of using that database, to just stick with S3, and you can do some interesting things with the paths that something lives at in S3 to take advantage of to handle that batching. The ultimate decision that I went with was to use S3 because of time constraints. I didn't want to have to deal with introducing new database tables, introducing what could be a complex architectural decision, because beyond just writing the code, you have to maintain it. And if I'm the main one writing it, I don't want to introduce a system that would be difficult for other people to pick up. So, I didn't go with using the database as a queue approach because though it is nice, it can be fancy. I think it's something that if someone else has to come back and look at it, you could eventually figure it out, sure, but it's not a common way people might use a database. It's not a common way that people who aren't very deep into databases might understand how to use it. So, I went with S3 for simplicity's sake and for also making sure other people could easily pick up and modify the code as needed.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Right. Yeah, that makes sense. Let's talk about, obviously, it feels like you were on a tight timeline working through this. I want to see how it was, like, how many people did you have to collaborate with? How many teams did you have to talk to, and how did that collaboration look like?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: For that particular project, building the submission application, I was primarily working with my team lead and a product manager. But I do have a few other examples I could talk about if you want to discuss working across teams. Following that, we were successful and focused on scaling, so I moved into a team lead role. In that role, I did a lot more interaction across teams, and there were a few ways I did that. Number one, I picked up the role as essentially a release manager. We grew the team, had a lot more people working on things, and a lot more changes going out. One thing I was responsible for was holding a weekly meeting where we collected all the changes we'd made over the past few weeks, consolidated them into a few key changes, and talked across our stakeholders to ensure people understood what was going out. In that case, I was talking with myself, other team leads working on things, product design, and also our customer support because they might have questions like, 'Hey, I thought this thing was working, but actually this happened,' or 'Hey, there's this new feature out, and I'm not sure how it works.' So that's one example where I had to work cross-functionally to understand what changes were going out, cut the releases, ensure we knew what was going out, and ultimately share with stakeholders how those changes might impact the service.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Got it. In terms of the scale of changes you were looking at as a release manager, what were some of the biggest challenges for you in that role, and how did you overcome those?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. I think there are a few challenges, especially when you have a large engineering organization, because of how we chose to do releases as a release manager. One of the biggest things is there are a lot of changes, and as a developer, you might be heads down working on your change and might not know when it's going out. So one thing I had to really work on is overcommunicating. That means not only making a scheduled event and calendar saying, 'Hey, this is when changes are going out,' but also talking with team leads and making sure they know the schedule. One thing I had to grow into was instead of assuming everyone knew we'd get the code out on Tuesday, I made an effort to put it on calendars, make meetings, and reach out to team leads to let them know when things were coming up. I also had to be accommodating because there might be a late-breaking change; for example, we release our thing to a staging environment, and it turns out it doesn't work as expected. There's also that coordination of making sure those changes get in if you have that bug fixed before anyone sees it. The other side of that is also on the product side. I think sometimes there are gaps between what I understand as a developer and team lead and what product might understand as what features are going out. One thing I had to work with as well was that initially, I thought, 'Okay, I'll just generate the release log from all the commits that came out.' As a developer, I can read them. That's not the same language that product managers or designers are reading. So I had to work along with our team leads to make sure we had our complete change log, walk through that, consolidate it, and translate it not just to what code was committed, but what changes were going on, like what product we were releasing each week. So I think communication was a really big part of what I had to grow into in that release manager role.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Nice. That's a great example. How much of that process was manual, and did you bring in any kind of automation into that?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So we had a decent bit of automation. I'm not reading through every single commit. GitHub has some tools where we can generate that would install automatically. One other thing that was introduced, which I won't take credit for, was that for adding backporting changes or late-breaking bug fixes, one of our DevOps engineers did a great job of adding some scripts to automate that. As I said, you want to make things as easy as possible for people, especially when you're trying to get out late-breaking changes. So we had an engineer introduce scripts to easily backport your changes from your branch to a release branch that we had cut, to simplify getting your changes out. As a developer, you might be heads down on something; you're worried about the bug fix, not about messing around with a bunch of Git commands to make your changes go where they need to go. So that was a bit of automation that was introduced as well.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So, the two projects we talked about today, if there were any changes you had the option to make today...

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: ...what changes would they be and why? Pick any project, any of those two projects.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: If there were any changes I had to make, what would I make and why?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And what would you change in that system today if you were given the choice?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: That's a good question. I think one improvement we could make, and again, this is more of a process thing, is trying to figure out a way to give teams more autonomy in release schedules. I think for a bunch of reasons, some of them team-level, some of them org-level, we went with a weekly release process where we send everything out in one big package. But if I could go back and change or improve the process, I'd want to push more of that dev-operations part down onto teams. Especially as you grow an organization, having that centralized place can be good in some cases, right? We have a weekly meeting, everyone is sitting down looking at every single change. But very often with bug fixes, you might not want to wait for a weekly release for everything to go out so that you can get your change out. Maybe you just wanted to deploy one piece of your application, or maybe your changes are completely separated from everything else.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that does make a lot of sense.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah. And

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: The one other thing I could add, if I may, was just that the other benefit is that developers have a better idea of what it takes to get their code from GitHub out to the internet, and that shared understanding is also beneficial.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah. Cool. Now we're at time. Thank you so much for taking the time to chat with me. This was a great discussion. I really enjoyed talking with you today.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Absolutely. I had a great time too.

training_pairs.question: Let's talk about something that you're proud of. Pick any technical project that you've done recently or maybe in the last couple years that you're really proud of and you're okay sharing with me today.
training_pairs.answer: One project I'm really proud of is the submission microservice. It's responsible for taking user data and sending it for processing, specifically to the IRS. I'm proud of it due to the tight timeline and the systems I introduced. I joined two months before launch and had to productionize everything. We focused on three key areas: recovery, ensuring we don't lose data; not overwhelming downstream APIs, like the IRS, to avoid rate limits; and overall performance. For recovery, we temporarily store data from a queue in object storage (S3) so it's available even if something goes down. For downstream API handling, we implemented an online-offline mode, similar to a circuit breaker, which pauses requests if errors occur and periodically pings the system to check its status. We also used batching to send multiple returns in a single request, preventing API overload. Ultimately, these changes helped us successfully file over 140,000 returns at the end of filing season without losing any information, even through outages or emergency deploys.
training_pairs.follow_up: I want to understand what was your role going into the project? You mentioned you were two months out from delivery. What was the state of the system when you joined and what did you specifically contribute to it?

training_pairs.question: What was your role going into the project? You mentioned you were two months out from delivery. What was the state of the system when you joined and what did you specifically contribute to it?
training_pairs.answer: When I joined, we had a basic system that could interact with downstream APIs, which was a starting point. My specific contributions included introducing the batching system. Instead of processing messages one by one from the queue, I implemented a system to queue them into batches, for example, of 100, and then send those batches to the API. I also implemented the offline mode, the circuit breaker pattern. The idea was that if we encountered errors during processing, like the API being down, we would pause our submissions and enter an offline mode. In this mode, we'd periodically ping the API to check if it was back up. Additionally, I was responsible for the error handling system that would pick up and process any returns that were held back during the offline period once the API became available again.
training_pairs.follow_up: Can you talk a little bit more about the API design itself? Let's talk about the offline system that you designed and implemented. What were the API constructs like, what were the agreements, and how did you think about coming up with those contracts?

training_pairs.question: Can you talk a little bit more about the API design itself? Let's talk about the offline system that you designed and implemented. What were the API constructs like, what were the agreements, and how did you think about coming up with those contracts?
training_pairs.answer: For the offline mode, we had to consider a few things. We have a microservice architecture using queues to decouple applications, so we're still reading messages from our queue, which contain the information to send downstream. The downstream API sends us an acknowledgment or an error. For supporting the offline mode, the first part was ensuring that even if the API is down, we're still pulling in that information and putting it into temporary storage. For this, I used S3. Specifically for the circuit breaker pattern, when you submit information to the API, it gives you either an acknowledgment or an error response. The IRS provides documentation on various error codes. We looked for specific error codes indicating a downstream issue. When such an error code was received, I triggered a piece of logic that would turn off submissions to that API. This meant we'd do all the pre-processing but hold off on the actual submission. The other part was the periodic ping to the API. We had a background job that, when offline mode was enabled, would wait a configurable number of minutes (e.g., three or five minutes) before pinging the API again. Once a positive response was received, I re-enabled our system to continue processing and sending submissions to the API.
training_pairs.follow_up: Just to understand where your service is in the entire flow, is it the first service that when a user uploads, does it go to your service first, or are there other services before they call into you?

training_pairs.question: Just to understand where your service is in the entire flow, is it the first service that when a user uploads, does it go to your service first, or are there other services before they call into you?
training_pairs.answer: There's one service before it calls into mine. Our architecture involves a web client that users interact with. When a user fills out a form and clicks submit, a request first goes to a traditional API. This API does some pre-processing and then puts a message onto an SQS message queue. My application then reads messages off of this queue, performs some processing based on requirements, and then sends that data directly to the API that handles the actual processing. There are other downstream services for things like rejection notifications or sending emails, but my service is the second step in the core flow: user submits, message goes on queue, my service reads it, processes it, and submits it to the final processing API.
training_pairs.follow_up: Was there a time criticality or an SLA that your service had to meet, like having to respond within so many seconds, or was that not time critical?

training_pairs.question: Was there a time criticality or an SLA that your service had to meet, like having to respond within so many seconds, or was that not time critical?
training_pairs.answer: In this case, we didn't have a hard SLA for my specific service. However, as a product, we aimed for users to receive a response within an hour. This is because the downstream API itself was asynchronous. When I submit data, it doesn't immediately confirm acceptance or rejection; it provides an acknowledgment that it has received the submission. Once we have that acknowledgment, we can later use the ID they provide to check the status – whether it was accepted or rejected. I'm not aware of any specific performance numbers published by that API, but we ensure we store the acknowledgment information so we can periodically check if they've processed it after giving us the initial acknowledgment.
training_pairs.follow_up: Did you have to make any architectural decisions while you were implementing and designing this stuff? And if so, can you walk me through how you made those decisions?

training_pairs.question: Did you have to make any architectural decisions while you were implementing and designing this stuff? And if so, can you walk me through how you made those decisions?
training_pairs.answer: Certainly. One key architectural decision I had to make was where to store the intermediate information. One option was to leverage S3 for both tracking returns that needed processing and temporarily storing the data. The other option was a split approach using a relational database for batching. With a database, you might have a table storing URLs referencing where the actual information lives, essentially treating the database as a queue where multiple instances read and process rows. I ultimately decided to use S3. My reasoning was primarily due to time constraints. I wanted to avoid introducing new database tables or a potentially complex architectural decision, as maintaining it goes beyond just writing the code. I didn't want to create a system that would be difficult for others to understand or pick up. While using a database as a queue can be elegant, it's not a common pattern for those not deeply familiar with databases. So, I chose S3 for its simplicity and to ensure that other team members could easily understand and modify the code as needed.
training_pairs.follow_up: It feels like you were on a tight timeline. How many people did you have to collaborate with? How many teams did you have to talk to, and how did that collaboration look like?

training_pairs.answer: Sure. I think there are a few challenges, especially when you have a large engineering organization, because of how we chose to do releases as a release manager. One of the biggest things is there are a lot of changes, and as a developer, you might be heads down working on your change and might not know when it's going out. So one thing I had to really work on is overcommunicating. That means not only making a scheduled event and calendar saying, 'Hey, this is when changes are going out,' but also talking with team leads and making sure they know the schedule. One thing I had to grow into was instead of assuming everyone knew we'd get the code out on Tuesday, I made an effort to put it on calendars, make meetings, and reach out to team leads to let them know when things were coming up. I also had to be accommodating because there might be a late-breaking change; for example, we release our thing to a staging environment, and it turns out it doesn't work as expected. There's also that coordination of making sure those changes get in if you have that bug fixed before anyone sees it. The other side of that is also on the product side. I think sometimes there are gaps between what I understand as a developer and team lead and what product might understand as what features are going out. One thing I had to work with as well was that initially, I thought, 'Okay, I'll just generate the release log from all the commits that came out.' As a developer, I can read them. That's not the same language that product managers or designers are reading. So I had to work along with our team leads to make sure we had our complete change log, walk through that, consolidate it, and translate it not just to what code was committed, but what changes were going on, like what product we were releasing each week. So I think communication was a really big part of what I had to grow into in that release manager role.

I think one improvement we could make, and again, this is more of a process thing, is trying to figure out a way to give teams more autonomy in release schedules. But if I could go back and change or improve the process, I'd want to push more of that dev-operations part down onto teams. Especially as you grow an organization, having that centralized place can be good in some cases, right? We have a weekly meeting, everyone is sitting down looking at every single change. But very often with bug fixes, you might not want to wait for a weekly release for everything to go out so that you can get your change out. Maybe you just wanted to deploy one piece of your application, or maybe your changes are completely separated from everything else. The one other thing I could add, if I may, was just that the other benefit is that developers have a better idea of what it takes to get their code from GitHub out to the internet, and that shared understanding is also beneficial.

training_pairs.answer: He mentioned where he's at now as a senior engineer. Where he's leading four engineers.

training_pairs.question: Jonathan, what are your thoughts on personal pitches? How do you approach yours? You included some hobbies in there at the end. What's your take?
training_pairs.answer: My take with the feedback is, yeah, I want to make it ideally as easy as possible for the interviewer to know who I am or jump on whatever. I like what you mentioned about breadcrumbs because now I'm thinking about, 'Okay, here are some other things I can add.' But yeah, on top of that, I think for me, it's just setting the scope, especially if you've worked at a few different places. If you don't have a big name brand on your resume, which I'm assuming your interviewer is going to have your resume at some point, you want to make it as easy as possible for them to know something. Versus if I said, 'You know, if I worked at a huge Fortune 500 company in data organization,' you can easily make assumptions. But if you don't, like I've jumped between so many places, I think you have to add those extra bits of details so people can kind of bring them onto something.
training_pairs.follow_up: Jonathan, thoughts on this bad boy? What do you think?

training_pairs.question: Tell me more about the API design. What were the specifics? How did you design and implement the offline system? What were the API constructs, agreements, and how did you think about coming up with those contracts?
training_pairs.answer: For the offline mode, we considered several factors. We have a microservice architecture using queues to decouple applications, reading messages from our queue to send information downstream, and receiving acknowledgments. For supporting offline mode, we store information in batches, ensuring we pull data into temporary storage (S3) even if the API is down. Using a circuit breaker pattern, when information is submitted, the API provides either an acknowledgment or an error response. We look for specific error codes indicating downstream issues. Upon receiving such an error, I initiated logic to turn off submissions to that API, utilizing an internal queuing system to hold off processing. A background job pings the API at configurable intervals (e.g., 3-5 minutes), and once a positive response is received, our system resumes processing and sending submissions.
training_pairs.follow_up: NONE

training_pairs.question: What was your reaction to Jonathan's explanation of the API design, specifically regarding the use of 'contracts' and what signals it sent about his seniority level?
training_pairs.answer: My initial reaction was that if the candidate had actually designed those APIs, they would have talked about specific request parameters and responses. The signal I got from Jonathan's response is that he probably worked on the queuing system around existing APIs, rather than implementing or defining the APIs themselves. I was specifically looking for experience defining schemas and contracts, like whether it was a REST API, GraphQL, or Protobuf, and how its definition looked. That kind of detail would have signaled experience in designing schemas and APIs across services, indicating higher seniority.
training_pairs.follow_up: NONE

training_pairs.question: What signals about leveling are shown when a candidate explains technical concepts like the circuit breaker pattern or queuing points, and then explains what they mean?
training_pairs.answer: I think it's a delicate balance. If a candidate is over-explaining something, it could mean they prepared that piece very well and want to provide all the details.
training_pairs.follow_up: Did Jonathan feel too scripted overall?

training_pairs.question: Did Jonathan feel too scripted overall?
training_pairs.answer: No, not overall. There was a part where he got a bit confused and lost his line of thought, which I felt might have been due to a conflict between something he had prepared and the current flow of thoughts. He had to pause, but I think he handled it well.
training_pairs.follow_up: NONE

training_pairs.question: How does handwaving details, like not remembering exact numbers or technology names, signal seniority in an interview?
training_pairs.answer: It's also a way of dissuading the interviewer from going into topics that you don't want to talk about.
training_pairs.follow_up: How do you use handwaving to dissuade an interviewer from certain topics?

training_pairs.question: How do you use handwaving to dissuade an interviewer from certain topics?
training_pairs.answer: Just like what Jonathan did. By saying, 'I'm not too sure what it was,' he signals that he's not comfortable talking about that topic, so I wouldn't pursue it for a follow-up.
training_pairs.follow_up: Should a candidate who hasn't designed an API but was involved in queuing just state that directly?

training_pairs.question: If a candidate has not designed an API but has been more involved in the queuing part, would you rather them just say that directly?
training_pairs.answer: Yes, that is setting expectations. It shows honesty and confidence. Especially during an interview, when someone exposes their vulnerabilities by saying, 'I don't know this,' it's a big sign of confidence that they can get things done. It also sets expectations that 'that's not my strength; this is the part I know and can talk about.'
training_pairs.follow_up: NONE

training_pairs.question: Jonathan, what are your thoughts on the idea of handwaving details and the 'counterpoint' of speaking with more authority, as demonstrated in the small clip?
training_pairs.answer: I think the point of handwaving, as you said, is beneficial. Watching it back, I realize there's a lot of preamble in my explanations. Getting to the point and reducing some of the 'education' part is definitely a benefit, especially in this case. In actual job experience, there were many times when you just wanted to get the thing done and reduce as much as you could. So, from an exposition perspective, I think it helps a lot to just get to the point and lean more on that side.
training_pairs.follow_up: NONE

training_pairs.question: If there were any changes you had the option to make today, what changes would they be and why? Pick any project, any of those two projects. And what would you change in that system today if you were given the choice?
training_pairs.answer: That's a good question. I think one improvement that we could make, and again, this is more of a process thing, is trying to figure out a way to give teams more autonomy in release schedules. I think for a bunch of reasons, some of them team level, some of them org level, we went with a weekly release process where we send everything out in one big package. But if I could go back and change or improve the process, I'd want to push more of that dev and operations part down onto teams. Especially as you grow an organization, having that centralized place can be good in some cases, right? We have a weekly meeting, everyone is sitting down looking at every single change, but very often with bug fixes, you might not want to wait for a weekly release for everything to go out so that you can get your change out. Maybe you just wanted to deploy one piece of your application or maybe your changes are completely separated from everything else. Yeah. And the one other thing I could add, if I may, is just that the other benefit is that developers have a better idea of what it takes to get their code from GitHub out to the internet, and that shared understanding is also beneficial.
training_pairs.follow_up: NONE

training_pairs.question: Jonathan, what are your takeaways from today?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, I mean, candidly, I kind of was thinking on my feet because I was like, 'Yeah, I did some prep, but I definitely wasn't expecting that question.' I also think, just in general, like you mentioned, as a senior engineer, the more you grow, the more you start to get into process stuff. And so I think I kind of, I didn't lean into that intentionally, but the reason I leaned into it is because honestly, it can be more frustrating for the process stuff versus a technical decision. Because it's a lot easier to get a technical decision right. Like I can go in my IDE, I can write the code. Yes, I might have to write it, I might have to talk with one or two people. But the process parts are where it gets kind of gnarly because it takes more than just you or even a handful of people to move that in any direction. Yeah, I'm going to leave it at that. I think you all have spoken to a lot of the other things.
training_pairs.follow_up: NONE
